items.correct: Rapid alternation between the written note and the note a step above.
items.why: Students confuse trill (rapid alternation between two notes) with vibrato (pitch oscillation around one note). Trills add ornamentation; vibrato adds expressive width to a sustained pitch.

items.q: How does a harmonic minor scale differ from a natural minor?
items.correct: The seventh degree is raised by a semitone (creating a leading note).
items.misconception: All notes are raised by a semitone.
items.why: Students think the whole scale changes. Only the 7TH is altered to create a leading note, which strengthens cadences in minor keys - producing the characteristic augmented second between 6th and 7th.

items.q: What does modulation mean in music?
items.correct: Changing from one key to another within a piece.
items.why: Students conflate modulation with any musical change. Specifically, modulation is a KEY CHANGE, usually established by a perfect cadence in the new key - a structural device.

items.q: Whose 1725 Gradus ad Parnassum codified species counterpoint?
items.why: Correct answer: Fux. “Rameau” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: Which famous four-note motif begins Beethoven's Fifth?
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Short-short-short-long. “Long-short-short” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: In what year did Bach compose the Goldberg Variations?
items.why: The real figure is 1741 AD. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
